[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/ba/Qzss-01-120s2.gif)](/wiki/File:Qzss-01-120s2.gif)

The **Quasi-Zenith Satellite System** (**QZSS**) ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 準天頂衛星システム, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *juntenchō eisei shisutemu*), also known as ***Michibiki*** (みちびき, "guidance"), is a [regional navigation satellite system](/wiki/Regional_navigation_satellite_system "Regional navigation satellite system") (RNSS) and a [satellite-based augmentation system](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (SBAS) developed by the [Japanese government](/wiki/Japanese_government "Japanese government") to enhance the United States-operated [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) in the [Asia-Oceania](/wiki/Asia-Pacific "Asia-Pacific") regions, with a focus on Japan.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The goal of QZSS is to provide highly precise and stable positioning services in the Asia-Oceania region, compatible with GPS.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Four-satellite QZSS services were available on a trial basis as of 12 January 2018,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and officially started on 1 November 2018.[[4]](#cite_note-gpsworld-4) A satellite navigation system independent of GPS is planned for 2023 with seven satellites.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) In May 2023 it was announced that the system would expand to eleven satellites.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 2002, the Japanese government authorized the development of QZSS, as a three-satellite regional [time transfer](/wiki/Time_transfer "Time transfer") system and a [satellite-based augmentation system](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (SBAS) for the United States operated [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) to be receivable within Japan. A contract was awarded to Advanced Space Business Corporation (ASBC), that began concept development work, and [Mitsubishi Electric](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Electric "Mitsubishi Electric"), [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi "Hitachi"), and GNSS Technologies Inc. However, ASBC collapsed in 2007, and the work was taken over by the Satellite Positioning Research and Application Center (SPAC), which is owned by four Japanese government departments: the [Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education,_Culture,_Sports,_Science_and_Technology "Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology"), the [Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_and_Communications "Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications"), the [Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Economy,_Trade_and_Industry "Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry"), and the [Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism](/wiki/Ministry_of_Land,_Infrastructure,_Transport_and_Tourism "Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism").[[8]](#cite_note-ASM2-8)

The first satellite "Michibiki" was launched on 11 September 2010.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Full operational status was expected by 2013.[[10]](#cite_note-ASM-10)[[11]](#cite_note-GPSWorld_TheSystem_Nov07-11) In March 2013, Japan's Cabinet Office announced the expansion of QZSS from three satellites to four. The US$526 million contract with Mitsubishi Electric for the construction of three satellites was scheduled for launch before the end of 2017.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The third satellite was launched into orbit on 19 August 2017,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and the fourth was launched on 10 October 2017.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The basic four-satellite system was announced as operational on 1 November 2018.[[4]](#cite_note-gpsworld-4)

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit), an eleven-satellite configuration is under consideration, which would provide redundancy for single-satellite failure.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Orbit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Orbit")]

QZSS uses one [geostationary satellite](/wiki/Geostationary_orbit "Geostationary orbit") and three satellites in [Tundra](/wiki/Tundra_orbit "Tundra orbit")-type highly inclined, slightly [elliptical](/wiki/Elliptical_orbit "Elliptical orbit"), [geosynchronous orbits](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit"). Each orbit is 120° apart from the other two. Because of this inclination, they are not geostationary; they do not remain in the same place in the sky. Instead, their ground traces are asymmetrical figure-8 patterns ([analemmas](/wiki/Analemma "Analemma")), designed to ensure that one is almost directly overhead (elevation 60° or more) over Japan at all times.

The nominal [orbital elements](/wiki/Orbital_elements "Orbital elements") are:

QZSS satellite Keplerian elements (nominal)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Epoch](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)") | 26 December 2009, 12:00 [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") |
| [Semimajor axis](/wiki/Semimajor_axis "Semimajor axis") (*a*) | 42,164 km (26,199 mi) |
| [Eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(orbit) "Eccentricity (orbit)") (*e*) | 0.075 ± 0.015 |
| [Inclination](/wiki/Inclination "Inclination") (*i*) | 43° ± 4° |
| [Right ascension of the ascending node](/wiki/Right_ascension_of_the_ascending_node "Right ascension of the ascending node") (*Ω*) | 195° (initial) |
| [Argument of perigee](/wiki/Argument_of_perigee "Argument of perigee") (*ω*) | 270° ± 2° |
| [Mean anomaly](/wiki/Mean_anomaly "Mean anomaly") (*M*0) | 305° (initial) |
| Central longitude of ground trace | 135° E ± 5° |

A planned seven-satellite constellation would consist of four Quasi-Zenith Orbit (QZO) satellites, two geostationary (GEO) satellites, and one quasi-geostationary (slight incline and eccentricity) orbit satellite. Ground trace of QZS-1 and -1R are positioned around 148° E. QZS-2, -4, -5 are around 139° E.[[17]](#cite_note-space20190123-17)

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit), an eleven-satellite configuration is under consideration. It would add two QZO satellites around 90°E longitude and two QZO satellites around 190°E longitude.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## QZSS and positioning augmentation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: QZSS and positioning augmentation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp/250px-GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:GPSTest_screenshot_(2025).webp)

Screenshot of GPSTest application showing a QZSS satellite and other GNSS satellites usage in [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") (2025)

The primary purpose of QZSS is to increase the availability of GPS in Japan's numerous [urban canyons](/wiki/Urban_canyon "Urban canyon"), where only satellites at very high elevation can be seen. A secondary function is performance enhancement, increasing the accuracy and reliability of GPS-derived navigation solutions. The Quasi-Zenith Satellites transmit signals compatible with the GPS L1C/A signal, as well as the modernized GPS L1C, L2C signal and L5 signals. This minimizes changes to existing GPS receivers. Compared to standalone GPS, the combined system GPS plus QZSS delivers improved positioning performance via ranging correction data provided through the transmission of submeter-class performance enhancement signals L1-SAIF and LEX from QZSS. It also improves reliability by means of failure monitoring and system health data notifications. QZSS also provides other support data to users to improve GPS satellite acquisition. According to its original plan, QZSS was to carry two types of space-borne [atomic clocks](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock"); a hydrogen maser and a rubidium (Rb) atomic clock. The development of a passive hydrogen maser for QZSS was abandoned in 2006. The positioning signal will be generated by a Rb clock and an architecture similar to the GPS timekeeping system will be employed. QZSS will also be able to use a [Two-Way Satellite Time and Frequency Transfer](/wiki/Two-Way_Satellite_Time_and_Frequency_Transfer "Two-Way Satellite Time and Frequency Transfer") (TWSTFT) scheme, which will be employed to gain some fundamental knowledge of satellite atomic standard behavior in space as well as for other research purposes.

### Signals and services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Signals and services")]

The QZSS provides the following classes of public service:[[28]](#cite_note-28)

* The PNT (positioning, navigation, and timing) service complements the signals used by the GPS, essentially acting as extra satellites. The QZSS satellites sync their clocks with GPS satellites. The service broadcasts at frequency bands L1C/A, L1C, L2C, and L5C, the same as GPS.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29)
* The SLAS (sub-meter level augmentation service) provides a form of [GNSS augmentation](/wiki/GNSS_augmentation "GNSS augmentation") for GPS interoperable with other GPS-SBAS systems. The principle of operation is similar to that of, e.g. [Wide Area Augmentation System](/wiki/Wide_Area_Augmentation_System "Wide Area Augmentation System"). It transmits on L1.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29)
* The CLAS (centimeter level augmentation service) provides high-precision positioning compatible with the higher-precision E6 service of [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"). The band is referred to as L6 or LEX, for "experimental".[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29)
* The MADOCA-PPP (Multi-GNSS Advanced Orbit and Clock Augmentation – [Precise Point Positioning](/wiki/Precise_Point_Positioning "Precise Point Positioning")) service is a L6 augmentation service independent from CLAS.
* The DC Report (Satellite Report for Disaster and Crisis Management) service broadcasts on L1S and provides information on floods and earthquakes.

The other classes of service are not publicly available:

* The PTV (Positioning Technology Verification) service broadcasts on L5S. The documentation only describes a "null" message type.
* The Q-ANPI (QZSS Safety Confirmation Service) is an authorized short message service.

### QZSS timekeeping and remote synchronization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: QZSS timekeeping and remote synchronization")]

Although the first generation QZSS timekeeping system (TKS) will be based on the Rb clock, the first QZSS satellites will carry a basic prototype of an experimental crystal clock synchronization system. During the first half of the two-year in-orbit test phase, preliminary tests will investigate the feasibility of the atomic clock-less technology which might be employed in the second generation QZSS.

The mentioned QZSS TKS technology is a novel satellite timekeeping system which does not require on-board atomic clocks as used by existing navigation satellite systems such as [BeiDou](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou"), [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS), [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS") or [NavIC](/wiki/Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System "Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System") system. This concept is differentiated by the employment of a synchronization framework combined with lightweight steerable on-board clocks which act as transponders re-broadcasting the precise time remotely provided by the time synchronization network located on the ground. This allows the system to operate optimally when satellites are in direct contact with the ground station, making it suitable for a system like the Japanese QZSS. Low satellite mass and low satellite manufacturing and launch cost are significant advantages of this system. An outline of this concept as well as two possible implementations of the time synchronization network for QZSS were studied and published in *Remote Synchronization Method for the Quasi-Zenith Satellite System*[[30]](#cite_note-TAP_dissertation-30) and *Remote Synchronization Method for the Quasi-Zenith Satellite System: study of a novel satellite timekeeping system which does not require on-board atomic clocks*.[[31]](#cite_note-TAP_QZSS-31)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]
